source_paper_title: Probabilistic Bounds on the k-Traveling Salesman Problem and the Traveling Repairman Problem
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Blanchard_2024, title={Probabilistic Bounds on the k-Traveling Salesman Problem and the Traveling Repairman Problem}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2021.0286}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2021.0286}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Blanchard, Moïse and Jacquillat, Alexandre and Jaillet, Patrick}, year={2024}, month=may, pages={1169–1191} }

Page 29, Section 6 (Conclusion).

However, directly using the TSP as subroutine would improve the constant 2 in the upper bound for the TRP to \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\), the constant appearing in the asymptotic length of the TSP. A natural question is whether this constant \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\) is tight. This would give an equivalence result of the TRP latency, as opposed to our constant-factor estimates.
title: Determine the asymptotically tight constant for traveling repairman latency under i.i.d. sampling
background_latex:
Let \(K\subset \mathbb{R}^2\) be a compact set, and let \(X_1,\dots,X_n\) be i.i.d. random points drawn from a distribution on \(K\) whose absolutely continuous part has density \(f\) (with respect to Lebesgue measure).

Given a realization \(V=\{X_1,\dots,X_n\}\), a (repairman) tour is an ordering \(x_1,\dots,x_n\) of the points, interpreted as the sequence in which they are visited, together with travel at unit speed along straight-line (Euclidean) segments.

For an ordering \(x_1,\dots,x_n\), define the latency (waiting time) of the \(i\)-th visited point \(x_i\) as
\[
\ell_i := \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \|x_{j+1}-x_j\|,
\]
with the convention \(\ell_1=0\). The traveling repairman problem (TRP), also called the minimum latency problem, minimizes the total latency
\[
L_{\mathrm{TRP}}(x_1,\dots,x_n) := \sum_{i=1}^n \ell_i 
= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} (n-i)\,\|x_{i+1}-x_i\|.
\]
Let \(l_{\mathrm{TRP}}(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) denote the optimal value (minimum) of \(L_{\mathrm{TRP}}\) over all orderings.

For comparison, the Euclidean traveling salesman problem (TSP) tour length on \(V\), denoted \(l_{\mathrm{TSP}}(X_1,\dots,X_n)\), is the minimum over cyclic tours of the total Euclidean length. Under i.i.d. sampling, the Beardwood–Halton–Hammersley (BHH) theorem implies
\[
\frac{l_{\mathrm{TSP}}(X_1,\dots,X_n)}{\sqrt{n}} \to \beta_{\mathrm{TSP}} \int_K \sqrt{f(x)}\,dx
\quad \text{a.s.}
\]
for a universal constant \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the constant \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\) is the asymptotically tight constant governing the leading-order growth of the expected optimal TRP total latency.

Concretely, decide whether one has an asymptotic equivalence of the form
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[l_{\mathrm{TRP}}(X_1,\dots,X_n)\bigr] \sim \beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\, n\sqrt{n}\, \iint_{K\times K} g_f(x,y)\,dx\,dy
\quad \text{as } n\to\infty,
\]
(or an equivalent characterization with leading constant \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\)), where \(g_f\) is the distribution-dependent integrand used to express the TRP scaling in constant-factor bounds.

Equivalently, determine the exact leading universal constant in the \(\Theta(n\sqrt{n})\) asymptotics for \(\mathbb{E}[l_{\mathrm{TRP}}]\), and whether it matches the TSP constant \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\).
latex:

\[

\[

Let \(l_{\mathrm{TRP}}(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) denote the optimal value (minimum) of \(L_{\mathrm{TRP}}\) over all orderings.

For comparison, the Euclidean traveling salesman problem (TSP) tour length on \(V\), denoted \(l_{\mathrm{TSP}}(X_1,\dots,X_n)\), is the minimum over cyclic tours of the total Euclidean length. Under i.i.d. sampling, the Beardwood–Halton–Hammersley (BHH) theorem implies
\[

\quad \text{a.s.}

for a universal constant \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\).

Determine whether the constant \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\) is the asymptotically tight constant governing the leading-order growth of the expected optimal TRP total latency.

\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[l_{\mathrm{TRP}}(X_1,\dots,X_n)\bigr] \sim \beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\, n\sqrt{n}\, \iint_{K\times K} g_f(x,y)\,dx\,dy

open_problem_quote: However, directly using the TSP as subroutine would improve the constant 2 in the upper bound for the TRP to \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\), the constant appearing in the asymptotic length of the TSP. A natural question is whether this constant \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\) is tight. This would give an equivalence result of the TRP latency, as opposed to our constant-factor estimates.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The conjectured tightness concerns a universal asymptotic constant, so it is not directly falsified by a single small instance. However, one can computationally estimate \(\mathbb{E}[l_{\mathrm{TRP}}]/(n\sqrt{n})\) for various densities \(f\) and large \(n\) to look for systematic gaps from \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\), providing empirical counterevidence or guidance.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary loop could search over constructive TRP heuristics (or lower-bound constructions) and use simulated i.i.d. samples to estimate the attained constant factor relative to a benchmark (e.g., the proposed \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\)-based constant). The fitness signal is computable via Monte Carlo, but proving optimality/tightness would still require nontrivial theory beyond discovered constructions.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper of Blanchard–Jacquillat–Jaillet establishes the correct growth order \(\mathbb{E}[l_{\mathrm{TRP}}]=\Theta(n\sqrt{n})\) for i.i.d. points in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) and provides matching (up to universal constants) distribution-dependent bounds expressed via an explicit integrand \(g_f(x,y)= f(y)\bigl(\mathbf{1}_{f(y)<f(x)}+\tfrac12\mathbf{1}_{f(y)=f(x)}\bigr)\sqrt{f(x)}\). Their constructive upper bound visits regions in decreasing density order and runs a (local) TSP-like tour within each region; the lower bound partitions space and uses k-TSP-type density/length tradeoffs to show no tour can beat \(\Theta(n\sqrt{n})\) scaling.

What remains open is identification of the exact universal leading constant in \(\mathbb{E}[l_{\mathrm{TRP}}]/(n\sqrt{n})\), and in particular whether it equals the Beardwood–Halton–Hammersley constant \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\) from the classical TSP limit theorem. The paper explicitly notes that tightening the TRP constant would require sharpening the constants in their k-TSP lower bounds (especially in regimes like \(k=\Omega(\log n)\)), suggesting that progress may hinge on a finer probabilistic-geometric analysis of near-optimal local tours and their interaction with density ordering.

Related work on minimum latency (e.g., Blum et al.; Goemans–Kleinberg; Sitters) develops worst-case approximation algorithms and PTAS results in Euclidean settings, but does not address stochastic i.i.d. asymptotics or identify the TRP leading constant. Thus, as of the provided materials, the constant-matching question with \(\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\) remains open.

sources.title: Probabilistic bounds on the k-Traveling Salesman Problem and the Traveling Repairman Problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves [math\mathbb{E}[l_{\mathrm{TRP}}]=\Theta(n\sqrt{n})[0m and gives distribution-dependent constant-factor bounds: [math c\iint_{K\times K} g_f(x,y)\,dx\,dy \le \liminf \mathbb{E}[l_{\mathrm{TRP}}]/(n\sqrt{n}) \le \limsup \mathbb{E}[l_{\mathrm{TRP}}]/(n\sqrt{n}) \le C\iint g_f\u001b[0m, with universal [mathc<C[0m. Leaves open whether the leading universal constant can be identified and whether it equals [math\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\u001b[0m.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open question: whether the TRP leading constant matches the BHH TSP constant [math\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\u001b[0m. It provides the current best constant-factor asymptotics and an explicit integrand [mathg_f\u001b[0m but does not determine the exact universal constant.

sources.title: The shortest path through many points
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes the Beardwood–Halton–Hammersley theorem: for i.i.d. points in [math\mathbb{R}^2[0m with density [mathf[0m, [mathl_{\mathrm{TSP}}/\sqrt{n} \to \beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\int \sqrt{f}[0m almost surely for a universal constant [math\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\u001b[0m.
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether the same universal constant [math\beta_{\mathrm{TSP}}\u001b[0m governs the leading term of TRP latency under i.i.d. sampling; BHH provides the benchmark constant for TSP.

sources.title: The minimum latency problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the minimum latency (traveling repairman) problem in general metrics; proves NP-hardness via reduction from TSP and gives early constant-factor approximation ideas via reductions to related network design problems.
sources.connection: Provides foundational algorithmic/complexity context for TRP, but does not address probabilistic Euclidean asymptotics or the leading constant under i.i.d. sampling.

sources.title: An improved approximation ratio for the minimum latency problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Improves approximation guarantees for minimum latency in general metrics by refining reductions/LP-based methods, improving constants over earlier work.
sources.connection: Relevant as algorithmic background for TRP but orthogonal to the probabilistic Euclidean constant question posed here.

sources.title: Polynomial time approximation schemes for the traveling repairman and other minimum latency problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives PTAS results for Euclidean and related minimum latency problems (including traveling repairman) in worst-case settings, extending approximation techniques beyond constant factors.
sources.connection: Shows Euclidean TRP admits PTAS in worst-case, but does not determine stochastic i.i.d. asymptotic constants; still provides methodological background.
